skill.name: shell-scripting
skill.description: Guidance for writing, debugging, and improving Bash and Zsh scripts with safer shell patterns, reusable templates, and command-line best practices.
skill.summary: Write safer Bash and Zsh automation with practical shell scripting patterns.
skill.author: CodingKaiser

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and heuristic findings, but review found they are shell scripting examples and reference patterns. No prompt injection, malicious intent, hidden credential collection, or unauthorized exfiltration was found. The skill is safe to publish with low risk because it teaches commands that can affect files or networks if users run adapted examples.

low_findings.title: Instructional shell command examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The shell command substitutions, backticks, xargs usage, and command examples are markdown reference material for shell scripting. They are not hidden runtime behavior, installers, or automatic execution paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The patterns appear inside instructional markdown or benign template variable assignments. Semantic review found no code that executes during skill loading or sends command output elsewhere.

low_findings.title: Network examples use public placeholder URLs
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The hardcoded URLs are source attribution and examples using example.com or api.example.com. No credential material or local data is sent to an attacker-controlled endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The README URL is attribution, and the network section demonstrates curl and wget syntax with placeholder domains. I found no evidence of exfiltration intent.

low_findings.title: Filesystem examples are normal shell references
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The /dev/null, mktemp, temporary directory, and /etc/passwd references are examples for redirection, temporary files, and awk field parsing. They do not read secrets for collection or transmission.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are common shell education patterns. The /etc/passwd line is still sensitive-looking, so this remains a low marketplace concern for user context.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography detections are substring matches
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography alerts point at lines containing words such as script, scripting, or usage text. No MD5, SHA1, DES, or insecure cryptographic implementation is present at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are descriptive shell script text, not cryptographic code. The scanner likely matched substrings inside ordinary words.

low_findings.title: Critical heuristic combination not confirmed
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The scanner combined command, network, filesystem, and credential-looking examples into a critical heuristic. Manual review found separate teaching examples, not a coordinated malicious workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The underlying signals are real text patterns, but their context is educational and disconnected. I found no prompt injection, obfuscation, credential harvesting, or exfiltration logic.

content.value_statement: Shell scripts often break on spaces, missing tools, and weak error handling. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code produce safer Bash and Zsh automation.
actual_capabilities: Explains Bash and Zsh script structure with strict mode defaults.
Provides patterns for argument parsing, logging, traps, and cleanup.
Shows safer file iteration with find, globs, and null delimiters.
Recommends modern command-line tools such as rg, fd, jq, and yq.
Includes a reusable shell script template for command-based scripts.
Highlights security concerns such as eval, quoting, rm -rf, and secrets.
limitations: It does not execute scripts or verify behavior on every target system.
Examples may need changes for macOS, Linux, Bash, or Zsh differences.
It cannot guarantee that generated commands are safe for production data.
It does not replace review by a shell or security expert.

use_cases.title: Create a reliable automation script
use_cases.description: Build a Bash script with options, logging, cleanup, and safer defaults for routine tasks.

use_cases.title: Debug failing terminal workflows
use_cases.description: Review quoting, globbing, exit handling, and command pipelines that fail across environments.

use_cases.title: Standardize shell script style
use_cases.description: Apply consistent patterns for argument parsing, functions, traps, and filesystem handling.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner script draft
prompt_templates.scenario: You need a simple script for a repeated task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a Bash script that does this task: [describe task]. Include strict mode, usage text, and safe quoting.

prompt_templates.title: Debug an existing script
prompt_templates.scenario: A shell script fails or behaves differently on another machine.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this shell script for bugs and portability issues: [paste script]. Explain each fix in simple terms.

prompt_templates.title: Improve production readiness
prompt_templates.scenario: A working script needs stronger safety and maintainability.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this Bash script for production use. Add argument parsing, logging, cleanup traps, and dry-run support.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced pipeline review
prompt_templates.scenario: A complex command pipeline needs security and reliability review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this shell workflow for command injection, unsafe file handling, race conditions, and portability risks: [paste workflow].

output_examples.input: Request: Create a script that processes all text files in a directory.
output_examples.output: A structured Bash script with strict mode, validated directory input, safe file iteration, logging, and a dry-run option.

output_examples.input: Request: Review a script that fails when filenames contain spaces.
output_examples.output: A concise diagnosis that identifies unsafe word splitting and recommends quoting, arrays, and null-delimited file reads.

output_examples.input: Request: Make a cleanup script safer before production use.
output_examples.output: A safer design that requires confirmation, validates paths, supports dry-run mode, and avoids broad destructive operations.

best_practices: Ask for the target shell and operating system before relying on platform-specific commands.
Use dry-run mode and backups before running scripts that modify files.
Review generated commands before execution, especially commands using rm, curl, eval, or xargs.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated shell commands blindly on production systems.
Do not pass untrusted input into eval, command substitution, or unquoted variables.
Do not store secrets directly in shell scripts or logs.

faq.question: Does this skill execute shell commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users or connected tools decide whether to run commands.

faq.question: Can it help with both Bash and Zsh?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers shared shell patterns and notes Zsh-specific features when relevant.

faq.question: Is it safe for production scripts?
faq.answer: It promotes safer patterns, but production scripts still need review, testing, and environment-specific validation.

faq.question: Can it debug existing scripts?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review scripts for quoting errors, portability issues, unsafe file handling, and weak error handling.

faq.question: Does it support macOS and Linux differences?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples that note differences such as sed, date, and stat behavior.

faq.question: What should users provide for best results?
faq.answer: Provide the shell, operating system, script goal, sample input, expected output, and any error messages.
